# **Product proposal: Fixeet**

# **Purpose**

The purpose of this document is to describe a software platform, Fixeet, for managing tasks involved in construction work (electricity, plumbing, etc) and related interactions between managers, workers and customers. The platform is primarily accessible as a mobile app but also available as a web application for managers and administrators. The app enables  to define, assign, dispatch, track and report tasks while giving visibility to customers on these tasks, and enabling interactions between customers and professionals (workers, managers, and contracting companies.

# **Problem statement**

Interactions between end customers and construction companies and their workers are often a very frustrating experience, leading to very low customer satisfaction, and a lot of wasted time for both customers and construction companies and their workers:

\-          Customers spend a lot of time and energy reporting issues and chasing after workers to ensure that work to be done is tracked, scheduled, and that workers come as planned, and that when they come, they complete all work as requested

\-          Companies, and particularly their managers, spend much time “managing” customers’ frustration and anger. They also spend too much time scheduling visits and chasing after their workers to ensure that they complete requested work. Given that there is no structured detailed list of tasks to be done, it is hard for managers to explain to workers what they must do, and hard for workers to remember what they need to do to complete their tasks

In summary, the list of tasks needed to complete work is not well defined, not structured, not tracked, and neither is their completion. There are multiple business consequences:

\-          Customers waste significant time and energy chasing after companies, workers, and trying to get visibility into planned work

\-          Customers are unFixeet, impacting the company’s reputation

\-          Workers often must come back multiple times to complete work: when they come, they usually have a subset of tasks in mind (usually those that the customer highlighted in their last phone call to complain). At best they will complete them, but the other tasks remain, and so the worker will have to come back

\-          Managers waste a lot of time and energy managing customers’ frustration

\-          Managers spend a lot of time scheduling multiple visits for their workers to come and complete tasks

\-          The lack of visibility on the complete set of tasks means that work may be planned in the wrong order, sometimes causing re-work or damage to existing work

\-          Chaotic work often creates many crises with customers, which in turn is generating a lot of re-prioritizations of assigned work, itself contributing to more frustration for all other customers that are not “prioritized” but instead suffer from delayed work.

 

# **The solution: Fixeet**

Fixeet solves the issues listed in the previous section by offering an application where customers, managers and workers interact together to streamline work.

## **Customers**

Customers can report issues with all necessary details for managers and workers to clearly understand the reported issue. An issue may include a textual description, images and videos.

They can also see the status of their issues and interact with managers and assigned workers. Interactions may be needed to clarify an issue, request for explanations, etc

They can also see when work on each issue is scheduled (or see that it is not scheduled yet).

## **Managers**

Managers manage tasks: they can create them, assign them to workers or contractors and validate worker’s task completion.

They also create Visits and assign Tasks and one or more workers to a visit. The scope of a Visit should be restricted to work related to a single domain (e.g. electrician, plumber) or contractor.

Note that the tool may also suggest a list of tasks and visits, based on (sub)project priorities, as well as tasks priorities and estimated tasks duration\[1\]

## **Workers**

Workers are notified of Visits they are assigned to.

They also have access to Tasks assigned to them, they can add comments, and change the task’s status (e.g. completed, more work needed, etc.)

 

## **Look’n’-feel**

The app manages projects and subprojects, tasks and visits. The tool is centered around the efficient management of tasks, as its primary focus: efficient communication among people involved in the task, providing visibility of tasks status, and efficiently assigning tasks to “workers’ visit” to optimize workers’ efficiency, while giving full visibility to customers, and ensuring a reliable schedule.

Users can view the list of tasks associated relevant to them:

\-          Customers can view tasks related to their (sub)-projects

\-          Managers can view tasks related to projects they manage

\-          Workers can view tasks they are assigned to

Users can enter a task ‘page’ to review details available, and chat with people involved in the task.

The app manages the task’s workflow:

\-          Manager review and approve submitted tasks and assigns a priority to each tasks so that work can be scheduled accordingly

\-          Manager create visits for sub-projects (apartments) and assigns worker to the visit

\-          Workers have a list of tasks that they need to complete during the day. The list of tasks is created by looking up the tasks associated with visits assigned to the worker

\-          Workers indicate when a task is completed

\-          Managers get full visibility of evolution of tasks completion

\-          Managers validate workers’ completion status (optional step)

\-          Customers get notified of tasks completion validated by managers (or by worker if previous step is not activated)

\-          A task may be reassigned to another worker

## **Side benefits**

Tracking tasks, their assignments and their completion, besides solving the problems described earlier, enables to identify how work can be optimized. For example, repeated complaints for specific tasks assigned to a worker may indicate that this worker needs to go through a training to improve his work quality for these specifics tasks.

With all communication between available in the tool, it becomes possible to show customers if their complaints are not justified.

# **Business model**

There are 2 possible models:

- Subscription-based, including variants, for example that the subscription includes a maximum number of activities (e.g. issue reports, visits, etc) and additional payments are required for usage beyond what is included in the subscription. Or different levels of subscriptions  
- Pay-per-use: User pays per activity (e.g. per apartment)  
- Hybrid: a subscription is required to access the platform, and usage follows the pay-per-use model

The application is targeting different categories of users. It may be necessary to offer different models to different categories of users. For example:

* The project owner pays for all people involved in the project: managers and workers of its company, but also contractors and customers. In this mode,

  o   contractors have only 1 account and cannot manage assignments to their own workers

  o   customers can only enter Fixeet for that specific (sub-)project. In other words, they cannot see a consolidated view of all their projects (assuming they are customers of multiple projects)

* Contractor has its own subscription  
* Customer has its own subscription, consequently, can see all projects he/she is part of when entering the application  
* Independently, a specific business model is offered to inspectors

# Competitive landscape

## Construction industry

In recent years, a number of project management platforms and applications have emerged for the construction industry, all focusing on streamlining and optimizing the construction phase. The next figure illustrates this focus, and mentions a number of companies covering this phase.  
![][image1]

Although not their focus, it seems that some companies have also understood the need to manage the defect liability period after delivery, and offer “modules”. Specifically, Fieldwire includes such support, and Cemento is reported to work on such extension.   
This is however not their primary focus, and the result is not user-friendly for this phase, and so far, does not include AI-support

## Claim management

A generic category of tools to manage claims in the field exist, with mature solutions. These solutions seem relevant competitors, but they all have a specific focus, sometimes barely overlapping with Fixeet.. Below is a list of such companies:

| Company | Overlap | Focus | Cost |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Reporto | High | Field data collection [https://reporto.co.il/](https://reporto.co.il/)(reported by [https://bhamim.co.il/](https://bhamim.co.il/) as customer) | ? |
| GoCanvas |  |  |  |
| BlueBeam | Low | Editing and managing documents, combined with measurement tools No data collection, no visit management | 23$/u/m |
| Fulcrum | Mid | Field data collection No coordination and visit management | 45$/u/m |
| FastField | Mid | Field data collection No coordination and visit management | 24-36$/u/m |
| PlanRadar | Low | Mostly a construction management sw | 35-180$/u/m |
| TrueContext | High | Customizable platform for data collection, report work, inform stakeholders Seems to cover all features but seems to come with complexity | 25-45$/u/m |

These tools seem mostly focused on the US market, but with growing and significant adoption in the EU as well.

#  Go-to-market strategy

## Defect management across industries

The need for a digital solution to collect field data in general and specifically to collect and manage is crossing countries and industries. Example of job categories that benefit from such a digital field defect collection and management:

- Building inspector \- quality control during building process  
- Real estate inspection (home, property portfolio, facilities etc)  
- Field service inspector \- inspects deployed equipment  
- Pipeline inspector \- corrosion, leaks, compliance  
- Site / factory acceptance test inspector \- e.g. for supplier qualification

An extended list is available in the appendix.

 The Fixeet concept emerged from real-estate experience, based on actual, personal painful experience and follow-up analysis of existing data. The intent is to remain focused on real-estate, at least for the foreseeing future, keeping in mind that the scope may be extended in the future.

## Real-estate

For real-estate, the initial approach has been to target construction companies, offering them to streamline the defect liability period with the measurable objective of improving customer satisfaction and reducing management and operational costs. We call this approach top-down: the real customer is the construction company, interested in streamlining issue management, therefore driving deployment to its customers and contractors. 3 approaches were identified to reach out to these companies:

- Innovation managers: these roles exist at large construction companies, and these people have the responsibility to look for innovative solutions. They are therefore in general responsive to inquiries. The drawback is that they are not the decision makers, and most often act as internal champions. The process is therefore somewhat lengthy and uncertain, especially given that multiple busy stakeholders are involved in the process. This approach is mostly relevant for startup companies looking for large lead customers, especially when funding is involved, and when construction companies are the only target customers. Given our past experience with AIgent, it is preferable to figure out another approach if possible  
- Customer relations managers: Fixeet helps improve customer satisfaction. These managers should therefore be incentives to improve processes that impact customer satisfaction. While this is true in theory, in practice, these managers have the primary responsibility to manage existing customers, not to improve processes, and they are also “trained” to filter any incoming request that defocuses them from this task  
- COO and similar roles have the responsibility to improve the company’s efficiency and should therefore be motivated to explore and adopt Fixeet. Reaching out to these people is however challenging

Fixeet improves productivity on 2 fronts:

- Collecting and reporting issues  
- Managing issues lifecycle and coordinating work towards their resolution

Construction companies are interested in the second category. The original idea assumed that issue collection would be done by apartment owners. Real-estate inspectors however have this sole responsibility and could therefore benefit from Fixeet by directly saving them time for each inspection. Analyzing how inspectors work in Israel, their work is fully “manual”: they go on-site, take notes on a notebook and photos with their smartphones, and when they are back home, they transfer their photos to their PC and write a report. This post-visit process likely takes a few hours. They then send a pdf report to their customer (usually the apartment owner) who in turn has no other means than manually tracking the issues with the construction company. We call this approach bottom-up, illustrating the idea that we drive Fixeet’s adoption from the companies and people reporting issues.   
This bottom-up approach has several advantages, and drawbacks, summarized below:

- (+) Easily quantifiable by customer (inspector), i.e. how much time do I save per inspection  
- (+) Not dependent on a broader adoption (as opposed to the top-down approach that requires all stakeholders to use the platform to get benefits), i.e. each inspector using Fixeet gets benefits immediately after an inspection  
- (+) Customer base are freelancers and small companies, easier to reach out, and easier to convince of trials (no lengthy decision process with multiple very busy stakeholders, nor legal threats typically associated with divisions by construction companies  
- (-) Customer-base is more fragmented, requiring more “leg work” to reach out. This is however not as bad as reaching end-user customers as it is still possible to create a list of these potential customers are they are identifiable on Internet (Google Maps, Midrag, etc)

## Countries

The go-to-market strategy should also consider the target countries.  
The idea emerged in Israel, because the founders live there (\!) and also because digital platforms for defect management are not deployed at all in this country: it is still a greenfield\!   
On the contrary, such defect management platforms have reached significant deployment in the US, and are being adopted, although with some delay vs US, in Europe as well.

For these reasons, we decided to focus on Israel for the initial deployment.

# Pricing

As discussed in the “business model” section, there are broadly 2 directions: all-inclusive subscription or pay-per-use, and hybrid options mixing them.  
When focusing on inspectors, given that they have an immediate benefit for each inspection, it should be possible to charge a fee per inspection. This approach has the following pros and cons:

* (+) cost directly linked to benefit  
* (+) revenue scale with usage  
* (+) overall revenue should be higher than an all-inclusive subscription, unless we charge a high subscription, which is likely not possible  
* (-+) charging per inspection may involve a more complicated charging mechanism in the app: we don’t want the inspector to be requested to pay for each inspection, so we likely need to setup an account in the app: the inspector credits (pays) some amount (e.g. 1000nis), and each inspection is charged from this account in the app (e.g. 100nis / inspection), and new inspection (apart creation) can only be done if there is enough credit in the app account  
* (-) inspector may get burdened by the fact that he will pay for each inspection (although this feeling may not be so strong if limited to the time he credits money to his app account)  
 


# Technical architecture

## Architecture overview

![][image2]

The architecture focuses on 2 interconnected sub-system:

- The application exposed to users, and connecting to a managed database  
- The automation of tasks, running on a separate computer, triggered by user actions and recurring tasks

Both systems share the same database to ensure consistency  
For the foreseeable future, we prioritize hosted solutions so that we don’t have to deal with scalability and maintenance of back-end systems. We however select technologies that could be self-hosted in the future, for cost-optimization purposes. Examples are:

- Open-source database with SaaS offering, such as Supabase  
- LiveKit voice kit with SaaS offering but with the possibility to self-host 

## Hints for the tool’s architecture

It needs to be possible for a company to contract work to another company. In other words, an issue is initially “assigned” to a company in charge of the work, but that company may have contracted out the work. It must be possible in the tool to assign a task to another company (that is, to a manager or worker of another company). So, a manager (or we might want to restrict this ability to the admin role of a company) must have the ability to declare another company as a contractor for a given project, so that it becomes possible to assign tasks to this company.

Tasks are always assigned to a project (e.g. a building).

A project may be subdivided into sub-projects (e.g. apartments within a building).

Customers are assigned to either a project, or one or a few sub-projects, and will have visibility on all tasks related to their sub-project

Ideally, all workers and managers of all companies involved in a project have accounts. It should however be possible to use the tool with partial accounts:

* Managers can ‘act’ as workers, and change state of tasks assigned to workers  
* If a company hired a contractor that does not use Fixeet, it must be possible for the manager to assign tasks and the contractor and manually change the state of tasks (like workers but in that case, the manager cannot ‘enter’ the contractor’s account and instead use a ‘proxy’ account or some similar concept)

Workflows between customers, managers and workers may need to evolve, and maybe even be tuned / adapted per project.

It may also be possible to create accounts on behalf of other people or companies, but the final account activation must be done by the person itself.

Most users are expected to be “low-end” users in the sense that they are not tech-sawvy, and have the objective to minimize the time they use the platform. The main consequence is that the user-interface should be as simple as possible, and likely “the more simple, the better”. Having said that, we also need to ensure that power users will have access to all information they need. The approach should therefore be that by default, a minimum set of information is displayed, but users’ preference will include the ability to select additional information they might want to see in specific screens/pages.

# Appendix: Field defect recording

Many industries need to identify, record and track defects. This section lists identified industries and inspection jobs.

# Construction & Infrastructure

These are the classics.

* **Building Inspector** – checks buildings for code compliance and defects

* **Construction Inspector / Site Inspector** – monitors works quality during construction

* **Clerk of Works** – represents the client, ensures workmanship quality

* **Quality Control (QC) Inspector** – verifies materials and execution

* **Snagging / Punch List Inspector** – inspects finished buildings before handover

* **Structural Inspector** – focuses on structural integrity (cracks, settlement, etc.)

* **Facade / Envelope Inspector** – walls, roofs, waterproofing, insulation

* **MEP Inspector** – HVAC, electrical, plumbing systems

---

# Real Estate & Property

Often mobile, checklist-driven, report-heavy.

* **Property Condition Assessor (PCA)** – evaluates property condition for buyers/investors

* **Home Inspector** – pre-purchase inspections

* **Property Surveyor** – assesses condition, defects, valuation inputs

* **Facilities Inspector** – inspects buildings under operation

* **Asset Condition Auditor** – portfolio-level inspections

---

# Industrial, Manufacturing & Energy

More technical, often regulated.

* **Quality Inspector** – inspects manufactured parts or assemblies

* **Field Service Inspector** – inspects deployed equipment

* **NDT Inspector** (Non-Destructive Testing) – ultrasonic, X-ray, magnetic tests

* **Pipeline Inspector** – corrosion, leaks, compliance

* **Wind Turbine / Solar Farm Inspector** – structural and operational checks

* **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) Inspector**

* **Site Acceptance Test (SAT) Inspector**

---

# Safety, Compliance & Risk

Defects \+ compliance \= liability.

* **Health & Safety Inspector** – hazards, non-compliance

* **Fire Safety Inspector** – alarms, exits, fire resistance

* **Environmental Inspector** – pollution, waste, land use compliance

* **Regulatory Compliance Inspector** – audits against standards

---

# Transport & Vehicles

Mobile, procedural, checklist-heavy.

* **Vehicle Inspector** – roadworthiness inspections

* **Aircraft Maintenance Inspector** – airworthiness checks

* **Railway Inspector** – tracks, rolling stock, signaling

* **Marine Surveyor** – ships, boats, offshore structures

---

# Insurance, Audits & Forensics

Defect detection after the fact.

* **Insurance Loss Adjuster** – site visits, damage assessment, reports

* **Claims Inspector** – verifies damage claims

* **Forensic Engineer** – investigates failures and defects

* **Risk Surveyor** – pre-insurance inspections

---

# Utilities & Public Services

Often large-scale, repetitive inspections.

* **Utility Inspector** – gas, water, electricity networks

* **Meter Inspector** – installation and compliance checks

* **Telecom Site Inspector** – towers, equipment shelters

---

\[1\] Not needed in the first release
